content.problemStatement:
You are given a list of strings strstrstr. Your task is to find the longest common prefix among all the strings in the list. If there is no common prefix, return −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input:

The first line contains an integer N, the number of strings.
The next line contain a string array str.




Output Format
For each test case, output the longest common prefix. If there is no common prefix, output −1-1−1.
constraints: All strings consist of lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of strings. The second line contains N strings separated by single spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the longest common prefix of the N strings. If no common prefix exists, print -1. Each answer is printed on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: all strings share the prefix "fl". Test case 2: the first characters differ, so there is no common prefix and -1 is printed.
Both strings start with "ab", which is the longest common prefix.
With only one string, the whole string is the common prefix.

solution.approach: We take the first string as a reference. For each character position i (starting from 0) we check whether all other strings have the same character at position i and are long enough. As soon as a mismatch or a shorter string is found we stop. The characters examined up to that point form the longest common prefix. If no character matches, the answer is -1. This works because any common prefix must be a prefix of the first string, and scanning left‑to‑right guarantees we find the maximal length.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·L) per test case, where L is the length of the shortest string among the N strings.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (ignoring the input strings).
